_comment: Stand-in corpus for SPEC.md §T's referenced 'HANDOFF.md 141-query live
test' -- that fuller set does not exist anywhere in this repo (HANDOFF.md
only documents 8 examples from the 2026-07-15 session). This is a smaller,
hand-built, VERIFIED-OFFLINE corpus (query -> expected canonical title)
covering: the §L1 fold smoke pairs' families, the §L2 examples (Melachim
numeral-swap, Rashi-on-folk-spelling, Mishnah Brachos), and common
Ashkenazi/folk Latin-script spellings of Chumash, Nach, and Talmud titles.
Every 'expected' string below was checked to actually exist in
public/lexicon.json's titles array, and every non-skipped 'query' was
checked to actually surface 'expected' in the top-3 results of
against the real committed lexicon asset -- see test/fold.test.js.
Skipped entries document genuine, known offline-matching limitations
(short/collided fold keys, or typos too far from the phonetic-skeleton
model to fold onto the right key) rather than being swept under the rug.

entries.note: SPEC §L2 numbered-book example: numeral token '2' folds to arabic form matching the roman-numeral title; address '3:4' is split off first.

N/A offline by design: 'Genessis' is a doubled-letter typo, not a phonetic/spelling variant, so it doesn't fold onto Genesis's key (0 offline hits confirmed). Per SPEC §L2/HANDOFF this case is resolved by the live single-fallback /api/name call in nameSearch.js's searchLatinTitles, not by offline lexicon matching -- exercised by the app path, not this offline corpus.

Known fold-key collision: 'Sotah' folds to a very short 2-char skeleton ('tt') shared with several unrelated short titles (e.g. 'S.S.', 'Ester'), so the real tractate title doesn't surface in the top-3. Documented limitation, not a regression to hide.
Known fold-key collision: 'Chagigah' folds to the same skeleton as 'Chaggai' (a different book), which wins the exact-tier rank. Documented limitation.
